content:
# Mark Chapter 6 — Disagreement Log
**Arbiter:** SOPHIA | **In council with:** EMET, LOGOS, PNEUMA
**Date:** 2026-03-05

This log records all verses in Mark Chapter 6 where the three translators (EMET, LOGOS, PNEUMA) significantly diverged in their renderings, along with SOPHIA's resolution and reasoning.

---

## 6:2 — "mighty works" vs. "remarkable miracles" for δυνάμεις

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "mighty-works through his hands come-into-being" | Literal; δυνάμεις as "mighty-works." |
| LOGOS | "How are such mighty works done by his hands?" | Formal; retains "mighty works." |
| PNEUMA | "how does he perform such remarkable miracles?" | Dynamic; "remarkable miracles" for δυνάμεις; drops "by his hands." |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "mighty works done by his hands" — "mighty works" is the consistent rendering for δυνάμεις and avoids importing later theological connotations that "miracles" carries. "By his hands" is textually present and important.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of EMET/LOGOS.

---

## 6:3 — "they took offense" vs. "they-were-being-scandalized" vs. "they refused to believe"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "they-were-being-scandalized at him" | Literal; preserves σκανδαλίζω. |
| LOGOS | "they took offense at him" | Formal equivalent; standard rendering. |
| PNEUMA | "they refused to believe in him" | Interpretive; shifts from "offense" to "unbelief." |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "they took offense at him" — σκανδαλίζω means to cause stumbling or offense, not simply to disbelieve. PNEUMA's rendering loses the scandal/stumbling-block metaphor.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 6:5 — "no mighty work" vs. "any mighty-work" vs. "any miracles"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "he-was not able there to-do any mighty-work" | Literal; "not able" for οὐκ ἐδύνατο. |
| LOGOS | "he could do no mighty work there" | Formal; same force. |
| PNEUMA | "He couldn't do any miracles there" | Dynamic; "miracles" for δυνάμεις; contraction. |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "He could do no mighty work there" — consistent vocabulary; preserves Mark's bold statement.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 6:8 — "no money" vs. "no copper" vs. "no money" for μὴ χαλκόν

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "no copper in the belt" | Literal; χαλκός = copper/bronze. |
| LOGOS | "no money in their belts" | Interprets χαλκός as currency. |
| PNEUMA | "no money" | Same interpretation; drops "in their belts." |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "no money in their belts" — χαλκός here clearly refers to copper coins. "In their belts" (εἰς τὴν ζώνην) is textual and evocative of first-century travel.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 6:14 — "they were saying" vs. "Some said" / "Some people were saying"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "they-were-saying, 'John the-[one]-baptizing has-been-raised'" | Plural; follows Sinaiticus variant. |
| LOGOS | "Some were saying, 'John the Baptist has been raised from the dead'" | Same plural reading. |
| PNEUMA | "Some people were saying, 'John the Baptist has come back from the dead! That's why he has such miraculous powers.'" | Expands with explanatory addition. |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "Some said" — PNEUMA's "That's why he has such miraculous powers" is already conveyed in the Greek but PNEUMA splits and amplifies the reporting. The expansion is unnecessary.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS's concise rendering.

---

## 6:20 — "greatly perplexed" vs. "greatly troubled" vs. "disturbed deeply" for πολλὰ ἠπόρει

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "he-was greatly perplexed" | Literal; ἀπορέω = to be at a loss/perplexed. |
| LOGOS | "he was greatly perplexed" | Same as EMET. |
| PNEUMA | "Listening to John disturbed Herod deeply" | Dynamic; "disturbed" shifts the emotional register. |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "he was greatly troubled" (SOPHIA's consensus) — a middle ground. ἀπορέω conveys bewilderment, and "troubled" captures Herod's uneasy fascination. Note: some manuscripts read ἐποίει ("he did [many things]") instead of ἠπόρει.

**Status:** Resolved. SOPHIA used "troubled" as a balance between "perplexed" and "disturbed."

---

## 6:22 — "his daughter Herodias" — textual/identification question

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "his daughter Herodias having-entered and having-danced" | Follows Sinaiticus reading αὐτοῦ Ἡρῳδιάδος. |
| LOGOS | "Herodias's daughter came in and danced" | Interprets as "the daughter of Herodias." |
| PNEUMA | "Herodias's daughter came in and danced" | Same as LOGOS. |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "Herodias's daughter came in and danced" — the Sinaiticus reading αὐτοῦ Ἡρῳδιάδος is best understood as "his [wife's], Herodias's, daughter." The daughter's name (Salome, per Josephus) is not supplied.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS/PNEUMA's interpretation of the genitive.

---

## 6:26 — "deeply grieved" vs. "exceedingly sorry" vs. "horrified" for περίλυπος γενόμενος

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "having-become deeply-grieved" | Literal; preserves participle. |
| LOGOS | "the king was exceedingly sorry" | Formal; "exceedingly sorry." |
| PNEUMA | "The king was horrified" | Dynamic; intensifies to "horrified." |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "deeply grieved" — περίλυπος indicates profound sorrow/distress, stronger than "sorry" but not quite "horrified." This same word describes Jesus in Gethsemane (14:34), and consistency matters.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of EMET's vocabulary.

---

## 6:31 — "a quiet place" vs. "a-deserted place" vs. "a quiet place" for ἔρημον τόπον

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "a-deserted place" | Literal; ἔρημος = desolate/deserted. |
| LOGOS | "a desolate place" | Same semantic field. |
| PNEUMA | "a quiet place" | Dynamic; focuses on the function (rest) rather than geography. |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "a quiet place" for v. 31 (where the purpose is rest) but "a desolate place" in v. 32/35 (where the geography matters for the feeding miracle). Context determines rendering.

**Status:** Resolved. Context-sensitive rendering.

---

## 6:34 — "had compassion" vs. "was-moved-with-compassion" vs. "his heart went out to them" for ἐσπλαγχνίσθη

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "was-moved-with-compassion upon them" | Literal; preserves σπλαγχνίζομαι. |
| LOGOS | "he had compassion on them" | Formal equivalent. |
| PNEUMA | "his heart went out to them" | Dynamic; English idiom for compassion. |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "he had compassion on them" — σπλαγχνίζομαι is the standard compassion verb, and "had compassion" is its established rendering. PNEUMA's "heart went out" is a legitimate English idiom but less precise.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 6:37 — "two hundred denarii" vs. "almost a year's wages"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "two-hundred denarii of-bread" | Literal; retains the monetary term. |
| LOGOS | "two hundred denarii worth of bread" | Formal; retains denarii. |
| PNEUMA | "It would take almost a year's wages to buy enough bread for this crowd!" | Dynamic; converts to economic equivalent; adds exclamation. |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "two hundred denarii worth of bread" — the specific amount is textual and should be preserved. Economic equivalents belong in footnotes, not the translation text, since they change over time.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of EMET/LOGOS.

---

## 6:39–40 — Seating arrangement vocabulary

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "to-recline all, group-by-group...section-by-section, by hundreds and by fifties" | Literal; preserves both Greek terms (συμπόσια συμπόσια, πρασιαὶ πρασιαί). |
| LOGOS | "to sit down in groups on the green grass...in groups, by hundreds and by fifties" | Formal; simplifies the doubled terms. |
| PNEUMA | "to sit down in groups on the green grass...in orderly groups of fifty and a hundred" | Dynamic; reverses the order; adds "orderly." |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "sit down in groups on the green grass" and "in groups of hundreds and fifties." The doubled Greek terms (symposia symposia, prasiai prasiai) convey organized groupings — one mention of "groups" suffices in English.

**Status:** Resolved. Minor stylistic differences.

---

## 6:48 — "He intended to pass by them" vs. "he-was-wanting to-pass-by them" vs. "He was about to walk right past them"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "he-was-wanting to-pass-by them" | Literal; ἤθελεν παρελθεῖν. |
| LOGOS | "He meant to pass by them" | Formal equivalent. |
| PNEUMA | "He was about to walk right past them" | Dynamic; "walk right past" intensifies. |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "He intended to pass by them" — captures the deliberate intention (ἤθελεν) while preserving the theophanic "pass by" (παρελθεῖν). PNEUMA's "walk right past" suggests dismissiveness, which misses the OT theophanic allusion.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS with slight adjustment.

---

## 6:49 — "ghost" vs. "phantom" for φάντασμα

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "a-phantom" | Transliterates φάντασμα. |
| LOGOS | "a ghost" | Common English equivalent. |
| PNEUMA | "a ghost" | Same as LOGOS. |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "a ghost" — more immediately understood by modern readers. φάντασμα means an apparition or specter.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS/PNEUMA.

---

## 6:50 — ΕΓΩ ΕΙΜΙ: "I AM" vs. "it is I" vs. "It's me"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "Take-courage! I AM. Do not fear." | Capitalizes as divine name reference (Exodus 3:14). |
| LOGOS | "Take heart; it is I. Do not be afraid." | Standard self-identification rendering. |
| PNEUMA | "Courage! It's me. Don't be afraid." | Colloquial; contractions. |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "Take courage. It is I. Do not be afraid." The Greek ΕΓΩ ΕΙΜΙ functions simultaneously as self-identification and divine self-revelation. "It is I" preserves this ambiguity — EMET's "I AM" resolves it too strongly toward the divine reading, while PNEUMA's "It's me" collapses it entirely into the ordinary.

**Status:** Resolved. A key christological translation decision. The deliberate ambiguity of the Greek is best served by "It is I."

---

## 6:52 — "their hearts were hardened" vs. "their heart was having-been-hardened" vs. "Their minds were closed"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "their heart was having-been-hardened" | Literal; singular "heart" (collective); periphrastic pluperfect. |
| LOGOS | "their hearts were hardened" | Formal; plural for English naturalness. |
| PNEUMA | "Their minds were closed" | Dynamic; "minds" for καρδία; "closed" for πεπωρωμένη. |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "their hearts were hardened" — "heart" (καρδία) should not be changed to "mind," as the biblical concept of heart encompasses will, understanding, and affection. "Hardened" (πωρόω) is the precise verb, carrying the Pharaoh/Exodus connotation.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 6:56 — "were made well" vs. "were-being-saved" vs. "was healed" for ἐσῴζοντο

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "were-being-saved" | Literal; σῴζω = "save." |
| LOGOS | "were made well" | Renders σῴζω in its healing/physical sense. |
| PNEUMA | "was healed" | Dynamic; straightforward. |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "were made well" — σῴζω in healing contexts means "to restore to health/wholeness." "Were saved" is too theological for the immediate context, though the double meaning is present.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## Summary of Disagreement Patterns

| Pattern | Occurrences | Typical Resolution |
|---|---|---|
| **Key theological vocabulary** (δυνάμεις, σπλαγχνίζομαι, ΕΓΩ ΕΙΜΙ, πωρόω) | vv. 2, 34, 50, 52 | Consistent formal equivalents preserved |
| **PNEUMA's modernizing substitutions** (miracles, minds, year's wages) | vv. 2, 37, 52 | Generally not adopted; precision preferred |
| **Register and tone** (contractions, colloquialisms) | vv. 50, 3, 37 | Middle register maintained |
| **Textual ambiguity** (v. 22 "daughter Herodias," v. 50 ΕΓΩ ΕΙΜΙ) | vv. 22, 50 | Preserved when possible; resolved when needed for clarity |
| **Context-sensitive rendering** (ἔρημον as "quiet" vs. "desolate") | vv. 31-35 | Varies by context |

**Overall Pattern Analysis:** Mark 6 is a long, narratively rich chapter with several theologically significant moments. The most consequential disagreement is the rendering of ΕΓΩ ΕΙΜΙ in v. 50, where the three translators represent three distinct christological positions: EMET's "I AM" (maximally divine), LOGOS's "it is I" (deliberately ambiguous), and PNEUMA's "It's me" (maximally ordinary). SOPHIA adopted the ambiguous middle position, recognizing that Mark's text sustains both readings simultaneously. Otherwise, the pattern continues: LOGOS provides the most frequently adopted base, PNEUMA captures narrative energy but over-interprets, and EMET's literalism preserves important Greek structures that inform the consensus without being adopted wholesale.
